did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a pretrained ResNet50 convolutional neural network (CNN) for the classification of histopathological melanoma images (distinguishing benign nevi from malignant melanoma) from H&E stained slides, comparing its performance against 11 pathologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' on the first page. It presents a comparative study where a convolutional neural network (CNN) was trained and tested on 695 histopathological melanoma images and its performance was directly compared against 11 pathologists. The paper includes specific sections for Background, Methods, Results, and Interpretation, reporting original experimental data and statistical findings (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, p-values).

title: Deep learning outperformed 11 pathologists in the classification of histopathological melanoma images
brief_summary: This study performed the first direct head-to-head comparison of a deep learning algorithm against human experts for classifying histopathological melanoma images. A pretrained ResNet50 convolutional neural network was trained on 595 cropped H&E slide images and tested on 100 additional images to distinguish between benign nevi and malignant melanoma. The CNN's performance was evaluated against 11 practicing histopathologists who diagnosed the same cropped images. The CNN achieved a mean sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy of 76%, 60%, and 68%, respectively, significantly outperforming the pathologists who scored 51.8%, 66.5%, and 59.2%. The results indicate that deep learning can surpass human experts in melanoma classification when limited image information is available.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class in the binary classification task. 345 melanoma specimens were included in the study dataset (695 total lesions). The CNN and pathologists were tasked with distinguishing these from nevi.

Benign class in the binary classification task. 350 nevus specimens were included in the study dataset. The nevi consisted of epidermal/junctional nevi and compound/epidermocorial nevi (1:1 ratio). Papillomatous nevi were excluded.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign nevus vs. malignant melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The study performed a binary classification task to distinguish between benign nevi and malignant melanoma using histopathological H&E stained images. The CNN predicted discrete class labels (nevus or melanoma) for cropped image sections, and performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics compared against 11 pathologists.

datasets_used.name: Private Dermatohistopathology Institute Dataset (Dr. Dieter Krahl)
datasets_used.description: A collection of anonymized H&E stained histopathological slides obtained from the largest regional dermatohistopathologic institute (Private Laboratory of Dermatohistopathology, Heidelberg, Germany). The dataset consists of biopsies from the past year, including nevi and melanomas. The ground truth labels were assigned by an expert histopathologist with over 20 years of experience.

use.training: 595 cropped H&E image sections from 595 individual patients were used for transfer learning to adapt the pretrained ResNet50 CNN.
use.testing: 100 additional H&E image sections were used to test the CNN's performance and compare it against 11 pathologists.
use.other: The original whole slides were digitalized and cropped; the expert histopathologist's diagnosis served as the ground truth.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the classification results (sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy) of the CNN against the 11 pathologists to test for statistical significance.
Used separately for sensitivity and specificity, and via a joint 2x2 table for overall correctness to compare the CNN and pathologists.
To determine statistical significance for the McNemar tests.
To visualize and compare the performance of the CNN against the pathologists by plotting the true positive rate (sensitivity) against the false positive rate (1-specificity).
To report the variability of sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy scores for the pathologists and the CNN across the 11 test runs.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in classification performance (sensitivity, specificity, or overall accuracy) between the deep learning algorithm (CNN) and the 11 pathologists.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in classification performance between the deep learning algorithm (CNN) and the 11 pathologists, with the CNN outperforming the pathologists.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The CNN significantly outperformed the 11 pathologists with a p-value of 0.016 (p < 0.05), indicating that the difference in classification performance was statistically significant.

Private Dermatohistopathology Institute.split: Test Set (100 randomly cropped H&E image sections, melanoma:nevi = 1:1)
Private Dermatohistopathology Institute.experiment: Comparison of ResNet50 CNN vs. 11 Pathologists

subgroup_experience_less_than_3_years.specificity: Not explicitly separated, but overall group specificity was 66.5%
subgroup_experience_less_than_3_years.accuracy: Not explicitly separated, but overall group accuracy was 59.2%

11 Pathologists (Group).subgroup_practicing_in_office_based_inst: 2 pathologists (specific metrics not separated from university group)
11 Pathologists (Group).subgroup_practicing_in_university_hospit: 9 pathologists (specific metrics not separated from office-based group)

Private Dermatohistopathology Institute.notes: The CNN achieved a mean sensitivity/specificity/accuracy of 76%/60%/68% over 11 test runs. The 11 pathologists achieved a mean sensitivity/specificity/accuracy of 51.8%/66.5%/59.2%. The CNN significantly outperformed the pathologists (p = 0.016). Only two of the 11 pathologists performed on par with the CNN. Pathologists with more than six years of experience had higher specificity (71.1% vs 66.5%) and accuracy (58.8% vs 59.2% - note: text says 58.8% vs 59.4% for accuracy in one place, but 59.2% is the group mean; the text states 'specificity and overall accuracy increased for the group of pathologists with more than six years of practical experience (58.5% vs. 71.1% and 58.8% vs. 59.4%)' which seems to imply a comparison between experience groups, but the group mean is 59.2%. The text explicitly states pathologists with less experience showed higher sensitivity (59.0% vs 48.0%). The McNemar test p-value was 0.016.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on the ImageNet dataset (approximately 1.28 million images with 1000 class labels)
architecture_used.input_size: Randomly cropped image sections (0.06% of the whole slide on average) with 10-fold magnification; specific pixel dimensions not reported

architecture_details.layer_configuration: Divided into three groups for differential learning rates: first six residual units, following eight residual blocks, and fully connected layers
architecture_details.transfer_learning: Used for adaptation to the melanoma/nevi classification task

training_details.optimizer: Stochastic gradient descent with warm restarts (SGDR)
training_details.loss_function: Loss function modeling the difference between predicted class labels and actual class labels (specific loss type like Cross-Entropy not explicitly named, but standard for classification)
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Differential learning rates: 0.009 for the first six residual units, 0.003 for the following eight residual blocks, and 0.01 for the fully connected layers. Cosine annealing method used to decrease learning rate stepwise. Warm restarts used to escape local minima.
training_details.training_data: 595 cropped H&E image sections (300 nevi, 295 melanomas)

classification_or_output_head.output: Binary classification (benign nevus vs. malignant melanoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Digitalization of H&E stained whole slide images using a NanoZoomer S360 digital slide scanner
Random cropping of image sections (averaging 0.06% of the whole slide) with 10-fold magnification
Selection criterion for cropping: visibility of the epidermis in the cropped area
conclusions: A convolutional neural network (CNN) using limited image information (randomly cropped sections of H&E slides) significantly outperformed 11 practicing histopathologists in the binary classification of benign nevi versus malignant melanoma (p = 0.016).
The CNN achieved a mean sensitivity of 76%, specificity of 60%, and accuracy of 68%, compared to the pathologists' mean sensitivity of 51.8%, specificity of 66.5%, and accuracy of 59.2%.
Only two of the 11 participating pathologists performed on par with the CNN.

Among the pathologists, those with more than six years of practical experience showed higher specificity (71.1%) and accuracy (58.8% vs 59.4% in specific subgroup comparisons mentioned in text, though group mean was 59.2%) compared to the group overall, while those with less experience showed higher sensitivity (59.0% vs 48.0%).
The study demonstrates the potential of deep learning to assist human melanoma diagnoses, particularly when only limited image information is available.
The outperformance may be attributed to the ability of artificial intelligence to mine 'sub-visual' image features that may not be visually discernible by pathologists.

Limitations include the use of randomly cropped images rather than whole slides, which restricts the context available to both the AI and the pathologists compared to routine clinical practice where whole slides and additional immunostaining are available.
The binary nature of the algorithm limits its ability to handle the broad spectrum of differential diagnoses that pathologists must consider in clinical practice.

The ground truth relies on a single expert's diagnosis, which may have a discordance rate of 25-26% compared to other pathologists, suggesting future studies should consider consensus diagnoses, genomic analyses, or cancer registry data.
Prospective studies using whole slides in clinical settings are necessary to confirm the clinical impact and utility of CNNs in assisting melanoma diagnoses.
important_remarks: The study used randomly cropped image sections (averaging 0.06% of the whole slide) rather than whole slides, which restricts the context available to both the AI and pathologists compared to routine clinical practice where whole slides and additional immunostaining are available.

The ground truth relied on a single expert histopathologist's diagnosis; the paper notes a literature-reported discordance rate of 25-26% between pathologists for this classification, suggesting the ground truth may not be perfect.
The algorithm was limited to a binary classification task (benign nevus vs. malignant melanoma), whereas pathologists must consider a broad spectrum of differential diagnoses in clinical practice.
Pathologists evaluated images on desktop screens which may have limited resolution below retinal resolution, potentially affecting their performance compared to the digital input of the CNN.
The dataset was sourced from a single institute (Private Laboratory of Dermatohistopathology, Heidelberg) and the nevi were randomly picked from a large sample, which may introduce selection bias.
Only two of the 11 participating pathologists performed on par with the CNN, indicating significant variability in human performance.

Prospective studies using whole slides in clinical settings are necessary to confirm the clinical impact and utility of CNNs, as this study was a retrospective comparison on limited image information.
